subtopic: Cross-source de-duplicated registry with aliases and provenance

compiled_from.source: Martin Fowler — PoEAA catalog (excluded from main list; scope mismatch — OO/ORM era)

category_legend.BLOCK: Building block — algorithm or data structure referenced in system design (not an architectural pattern per se)

patterns.name: Monolithic Architecture
patterns.summary: Single deployable unit containing all app concerns. Baseline against which microservices/SOA are contrasted.

patterns.name: Microservices Architecture
patterns.summary: App composed of small independently deployable services aligned to business capabilities.

patterns.name: Service-Oriented Architecture (SOA)
patterns.summary: Coarser-grained services typically integrated through an enterprise service bus (predecessor to microservices).

patterns.name: Event-Driven Architecture
patterns.summary: Services react to events on a shared bus rather than being called directly. Enables loose coupling and async fan-out.

patterns.name: Serverless / Functions-as-a-Service
patterns.summary: Workloads packaged as short-lived event-driven functions; provider manages scaling and idle cost.

patterns.name: Layered (N-Tier) Architecture
patterns.summary: Strict horizontal layers (presentation/business/data); each layer only talks to the one below.

patterns.name: Hexagonal / Ports & Adapters
patterns.summary: Domain core isolated from delivery mechanisms (DB/UI/queues) via ports + adapters.

patterns.name: Space-Based Architecture
patterns.summary: In-memory data grid shared across processing units; eliminates DB bottleneck for high-volume burst workloads.

patterns.name: Pipes and Filters
patterns.summary: Decompose processing into a chain of independent stages connected by channels.

patterns.name: Strangler Fig
patterns.summary: Incrementally replace a legacy system by routing more functionality to a new implementation behind a facade.

patterns.name: Anti-Corruption Layer
patterns.summary: Translation layer that isolates a new model from a legacy/external system's model.

patterns.name: Decompose by Business Capability
patterns.summary: Define service boundaries by what the business does (capability map).

patterns.name: Decompose by Subdomain
patterns.summary: Define service boundaries by DDD subdomains (core/supporting/generic).

patterns.name: Self-Contained Service
patterns.summary: A service owns its UI, logic, and data — no synchronous runtime dependency on other SCSs.

patterns.name: Ambassador
patterns.summary: Helper process colocated with the client that handles cross-cutting client-side concerns (TLS, retries, routing).

patterns.name: Sidecar
patterns.summary: Helper process colocated with a service that handles cross-cutting concerns (logging, proxy, config).

patterns.name: Service Mesh
patterns.summary: Dedicated infra layer (typically Envoy sidecars + control plane) for service-to-service traffic management, mTLS, observability.

patterns.name: Database per Service
patterns.summary: Each microservice owns its own private database; no cross-service joins.

patterns.name: Shared Database (anti-pattern reference)
patterns.summary: Multiple services share one DB. Listed as a known compromise; usually an anti-pattern in microservices.

patterns.name: CQRS
patterns.summary: Separate read and write models so each can be optimized independently.

patterns.name: Event Sourcing
patterns.summary: Persist state as an append-only log of domain events; current state is derived by replay.

patterns.name: Saga
patterns.summary: Maintain consistency across services via a sequence of local transactions and compensating actions.

patterns.name: Transactional Outbox
patterns.summary: Atomically write business state and a message-to-publish to the same DB, then ship messages from the outbox.

patterns.name: Transaction Log Tailing
patterns.summary: Stream changes by tailing the DB transaction log (e.g., Debezium reading MySQL binlog/Postgres WAL).

patterns.name: Polling Publisher
patterns.summary: Periodically poll the outbox table to publish messages (simpler alternative to log tailing).

patterns.name: Materialized View
patterns.summary: Pre-computed read-optimized projection over source data; updated on write or schedule.

patterns.name: Index Table
patterns.summary: Maintain secondary indexes over data in stores that don't natively support them well (e.g., KV stores).

patterns.name: Sharding
patterns.summary: Split data across multiple instances by a partition key to scale beyond single-node limits.

patterns.name: Federation
patterns.summary: Split data by function/domain (e.g., users-DB, products-DB) instead of by row.

patterns.name: Leader-Follower Replication
patterns.summary: Writes go to leader; followers replicate asynchronously and serve reads. Postgres streaming replication, MySQL replication, MongoDB replica sets.

patterns.name: Key-Range Partitions
patterns.summary: Partition by sorted key ranges for efficient range scans. HBase, Bigtable, Spanner. Vs hash partitioning.

patterns.name: Fixed Partitions
patterns.summary: Fix partition count at creation; rebalance by reassigning whole partitions. Kafka topics, Elasticsearch shards.

patterns.name: Follower Reads
patterns.summary: Serve reads from followers for higher throughput / lower latency, accepting bounded staleness.

patterns.name: Versioned Value (MVCC)
patterns.summary: Store every update with a new version; enables snapshot reads, conflict detection. Postgres, Spanner, CockroachDB, MySQL InnoDB.

patterns.name: Replicated Log
patterns.summary: Identical totally-ordered log on every replica; state machines deterministically derived from it. Raft log, Kafka log, BookKeeper.

patterns.name: Segmented Log
patterns.summary: Split append-only log into bounded-size segments for easier rotation, deletion, compaction. Kafka log segments, Cassandra commitlog.

patterns.name: High-Water Mark
patterns.summary: Offset in replicated log up to which all replicas have ack'd; readers can only see up to this point. Kafka HWM, Raft commitIndex.

patterns.name: Low-Water Mark
patterns.summary: Offset below which the log can be safely garbage-collected. Kafka retention, Raft snapshots.

patterns.name: Multi-Leader Replication
patterns.summary: Multiple writable replicas; conflict resolution required.

patterns.name: Read-Write Split
patterns.summary: Route reads to replicas, writes to primary. Operational pattern, not architectural CQRS.

patterns.name: Denormalization
patterns.summary: Duplicate data across tables/docs to avoid joins and improve read latency.

patterns.name: CRDT
patterns.summary: Data types whose concurrent updates always converge without coordination (G-Counter, OR-Set, etc.).

patterns.name: Polyglot Persistence
patterns.summary: Use different DBs for different needs (SQL for transactions, KV for cache, search index, time-series, graph).

patterns.name: Valet Key
patterns.summary: Issue short-lived signed tokens granting direct, limited access to a backend resource (e.g., S3 presigned URL).

patterns.name: Cache-Aside
patterns.summary: App checks cache; on miss, loads from DB and populates cache.

patterns.name: Read-Through Cache
patterns.summary: Cache itself loads from DB on miss; app only talks to cache.

patterns.name: Write-Through Cache
patterns.summary: Writes go to cache and DB synchronously.

patterns.name: Write-Behind Cache
patterns.summary: Writes go to cache immediately; DB updated asynchronously.

patterns.name: Refresh-Ahead Cache
patterns.summary: Proactively refresh popular cache entries before they expire.

patterns.name: Static Content Hosting / CDN
patterns.summary: Serve static (or cacheable dynamic) content from geo-distributed edge nodes close to users.

patterns.name: API Gateway
patterns.summary: Single entry point for clients; handles routing, auth, rate-limiting, aggregation.

patterns.name: Backend for Frontend
patterns.summary: Separate backend per frontend (web/iOS/Android) tailored to that client's needs.

patterns.name: Gateway Aggregation
patterns.summary: Gateway combines multiple backend calls into one response for the client.

patterns.name: Gateway Offloading
patterns.summary: Move shared concerns (TLS, auth, compression) from services to the gateway.

patterns.name: Gateway Routing
patterns.summary: Route requests to backend services via a single endpoint (path/host/header-based).

patterns.name: Publish-Subscribe
patterns.summary: Publishers emit messages to topics; subscribers receive without knowing publishers.

patterns.name: Asynchronous Request-Reply
patterns.summary: Decouple slow backend work via message queue; client polls or is notified when complete.

patterns.name: Competing Consumers
patterns.summary: Multiple consumers pull from same queue to parallelize work.

patterns.name: Priority Queue
patterns.summary: Messages processed in priority order rather than FIFO.

patterns.name: Claim Check
patterns.summary: Store large payload in object store; pass reference through the queue.

patterns.name: Choreography
patterns.summary: Services react to shared events without a central coordinator.

patterns.name: Scheduler-Agent-Supervisor
patterns.summary: Coordinate distributed long-running work; supervisor detects failures and triggers compensation.

patterns.name: WebSocket (full-duplex)
patterns.summary: Persistent bidirectional TCP channel for real-time comms (chat, live data).

patterns.name: Server-Sent Events
patterns.summary: Server pushes updates over a long-lived HTTP response; one-way (server→client).

patterns.name: Long Polling
patterns.summary: Client holds open HTTP request until server has data or timeout — predecessor to SSE/WebSocket.

patterns.name: Webhook
patterns.summary: Producer POSTs to a consumer-registered URL when events occur — inverted polling.

patterns.name: Client-Side Service Discovery
patterns.summary: Client queries registry, then load-balances itself.

patterns.name: Server-Side Service Discovery
patterns.summary: Client hits an LB/router that queries registry and forwards.

patterns.name: Service Registry
patterns.summary: Central catalog of service instances and their network locations (Consul, etcd, Eureka, ZK).

patterns.name: Self-Registration
patterns.summary: Service instance registers/deregisters itself with the registry.

patterns.name: 3rd-Party Registration
patterns.summary: An external registrar tracks instances (e.g., Kubernetes controllers).

patterns.name: Circuit Breaker
patterns.summary: Stop calling a failing dependency to let it recover; trip open after N failures, half-open to test.

patterns.name: Retry
patterns.summary: Re-issue failed transient operations with backoff + jitter; bounded attempts.

patterns.name: Timeout
patterns.summary: Cap how long a call may take; never block indefinitely on a dependency.

patterns.name: Bulkhead
patterns.summary: Isolate resources (thread pools/connections) per dependency so one failure doesn't sink the whole process.

patterns.name: Throttling / Rate Limiting
patterns.summary: Enforce per-client/global request budgets; reject or queue excess traffic.

patterns.name: Backpressure
patterns.summary: Downstream signals upstream to slow down when overloaded; prevents queue blow-up.

patterns.name: Compensating Transaction
patterns.summary: Undo a completed step via a semantically inverse action (refund vs. rollback).

patterns.name: Idempotency Key / Idempotent Receiver
patterns.summary: Deduplicate retried requests using a client-supplied key persisted on the server. Stripe API, AWS SDK retries.

patterns.name: Dead Letter Queue
patterns.summary: Park unprocessable messages in a separate queue for inspection and replay.

patterns.name: Health Endpoint Monitoring
patterns.summary: Expose /health (liveness/readiness) endpoints for orchestrators and load balancers. Pull-based.

patterns.name: Heartbeat
patterns.summary: Periodic 'I'm alive' push from node to peers/leader; absence signals failure. Raft heartbeats, Kafka controller, ZK sessions. Push-based (vs Health Endpoint pull).

patterns.name: Leader Election
patterns.summary: Elect one instance as coordinator; failover on loss (often via Raft/ZK/etcd).

patterns.name: Queue-Based Load Leveling
patterns.summary: Queue absorbs spikes so workers process at steady rate, protecting downstream services.

patterns.name: Compute Resource Consolidation
patterns.summary: Pack multiple tasks/services on shared compute to improve utilization.

patterns.name: Load Balancing
patterns.summary: Distribute traffic across instances by algorithm; L4 is TCP, L7 is HTTP-aware.

patterns.name: Consistent Hashing
patterns.summary: Hash ring that minimizes key movement when nodes are added/removed. Used by Dynamo-style stores, CDNs, sharded caches.

patterns.name: Geo-Distribution / Geo-Sharding
patterns.summary: Replicate/partition by region for latency and disaster-recovery.

patterns.name: Auto-Scaling
patterns.summary: Add/remove instances based on metrics (CPU, queue depth, custom signals).

patterns.name: Edge Computing
patterns.summary: Run compute at CDN edge (Cloudflare Workers, Lambda@Edge) for low-latency dynamic responses.

patterns.name: Reverse Proxy
patterns.summary: Front-line proxy that terminates client connections and forwards to backends (NGINX, HAProxy, Envoy).

patterns.name: Optimistic Locking
patterns.summary: Detect conflict at commit via version/timestamp; retry on conflict.

patterns.name: Pessimistic Locking
patterns.summary: Acquire exclusive lock before reading/modifying; blocks other writers.

patterns.name: Distributed Lock
patterns.summary: Mutual exclusion across processes (Redis Redlock, ZooKeeper, etcd) — fraught with edge cases.

patterns.name: Two-Phase Commit
patterns.summary: Coordinator drives all participants through prepare + commit phases. Blocking on coordinator failure.

patterns.name: Raft Consensus
patterns.summary: Leader-based consensus algorithm. etcd, Consul, CockroachDB, TiKV, RabbitMQ quorum queues. Constituent Joshi patterns: Replicated Log + Generation Clock + Majority Quorum + Heartbeat.

patterns.name: Paxos Consensus
patterns.summary: Foundational consensus algorithm. Google Chubby, Spanner, Megastore. Cassandra lightweight transactions.

patterns.name: Lamport Clock
patterns.summary: Scalar counter that captures happens-before ordering across processes.

patterns.name: Vector Clock / Version Vector
patterns.summary: Per-process counter vector that detects causal vs concurrent events. Joshi distinguishes Version Vector (on values) from Vector Clock (on events); commonly conflated. Dynamo, Riak, Voldemort.

patterns.name: Hybrid Logical Clock
patterns.summary: Pair physical time with logical counter for monotonic + near-real-time ordering. CockroachDB, YugabyteDB, MongoDB.

patterns.name: Clock-Bound Wait
patterns.summary: Wait out maximum clock uncertainty before commit so timestamps are globally ordered. Google Spanner TrueTime.

patterns.name: Quorum / Majority Quorum
patterns.summary: Dynamo-style consistency tuning (R+W>N); or strict majority requirement so two disjoint groups can't both progress (Raft, Paxos).

patterns.name: Lease
patterns.summary: Time-bounded grant of a right (lock, leadership, cache validity). Auto-expires on holder failure. Chubby, ZK ephemeral nodes, etcd leases.

patterns.name: Emergent Leader
patterns.summary: Pick leader implicitly by age in cluster (oldest live node wins) instead of explicit election. SWIM, Hashicorp Serf.

patterns.name: Generation Clock
patterns.summary: Monotonic number that increments on leadership/config change; lets followers reject stale leaders. Raft term, Kafka leader epoch, ZK zxid.

patterns.name: Singular Update Queue
patterns.summary: Single thread processes updates from a queue; eliminates locks. LMAX Disruptor, Node.js, Redis, Kafka controller.

patterns.name: State Watch
patterns.summary: Clients subscribe to changes on specific keys/paths; server notifies on change. ZooKeeper watches, etcd watches, Consul blocking queries.

patterns.name: Consistent Core
patterns.summary: Run small strongly-consistent control cluster (ZK/etcd) alongside larger eventually-consistent data cluster. HBase, pre-KRaft Kafka, Mesos.

patterns.name: Bloom Filter
patterns.summary: Probabilistic set membership; no false negatives, tunable false positives. Used in LSM trees, web crawlers, dedup.

patterns.name: HyperLogLog
patterns.summary: Probabilistic distinct-count estimation in tiny memory. Used in Redis PFCOUNT, Google BigQuery.

patterns.name: Count-Min Sketch
patterns.summary: Probabilistic frequency estimation; overestimates by tunable amount.

patterns.name: Trie
patterns.summary: Tree keyed by string prefixes; used for autocomplete, IP routing.

patterns.name: Write-Ahead Log
patterns.summary: Append-only log written before applying state changes; basis of crash recovery in all major DBs. Postgres WAL, MySQL redo log, SQLite journal, RocksDB.

patterns.name: LSM Tree
patterns.summary: Write-optimized storage engine; memtable + sorted SSTables + compaction. RocksDB, Cassandra, HBase, LevelDB, ScyllaDB.

patterns.name: B-Tree / B+ Tree
patterns.summary: Read-optimized self-balancing tree; classic OLTP index. Postgres, MySQL InnoDB, SQLite, SQL Server.

patterns.name: Geohash / R-Tree
patterns.summary: Encode 2D coords as 1D string (Geohash) or tree (R-Tree, Quadtree) for proximity search.

patterns.name: Merkle Tree
patterns.summary: Tree of hashes for efficient diffing/verification of large datasets. Used in Git, Dynamo anti-entropy, blockchains.

patterns.name: Gossip Protocol
patterns.summary: Nodes randomly exchange state with peers; eventually consistent cluster membership. Cassandra, Consul, Dynamo, SWIM.

patterns.name: Blue-Green Deployment
patterns.summary: Two identical environments; flip traffic atomically; instant rollback by flipping back.

patterns.name: Canary Release
patterns.summary: Roll new version to small % of traffic first, observe metrics, then ramp up.

patterns.name: Feature Flag / Toggle
patterns.summary: Decouple deploy from release; toggle features at runtime per user/cohort.

patterns.name: External Configuration Store
patterns.summary: Move config out of code/image into a central store (Consul, AppConfig, Spring Cloud Config).

patterns.name: Microservice Chassis
patterns.summary: Framework that handles cross-cutting concerns (logging, metrics, config, discovery) for every service.

patterns.name: Service Template
patterns.summary: Templated repo for new services so all teams start from the same baseline.

patterns.name: Deployment Stamps
patterns.summary: Independent replicas of the full stack per tenant/region; horizontal scaling by adding stamps.

patterns.name: Geode
patterns.summary: Deploy collections of backend services into geo-regions, each serving any request — for global low-latency apps.

patterns.name: Distributed Tracing
patterns.summary: Propagate trace/span IDs across services to reconstruct request paths.

patterns.name: Log Aggregation
patterns.summary: Ship logs from all instances to a central searchable store (ELK, Loki, Splunk).

patterns.name: Application Metrics
patterns.summary: Emit counters/gauges/histograms (Prometheus, StatsD) for SLO monitoring and autoscaling.

patterns.name: Audit Logging
patterns.summary: Tamper-evident log of security-/business-relevant events.

patterns.name: Correlation ID
patterns.summary: Propagate a unique ID through all logs/spans for a single request.

patterns.name: Exception Tracking
patterns.summary: Aggregate, deduplicate, and alert on uncaught exceptions (Sentry, Rollbar).

patterns.name: Federated Identity
patterns.summary: Delegate authentication to a trusted external IdP; relying parties accept tokens.

patterns.name: Gatekeeper
patterns.summary: Dedicated host that brokers between clients and a trusted backend, validating/sanitizing all input.

patterns.name: Access Token
patterns.summary: Signed token carries identity/scopes; services verify locally without IdP roundtrip.

patterns.name: Consumer-Driven Contract Test
patterns.summary: Consumer writes the contract; provider tests against it in CI to prevent breaking changes.

patterns.name: Service Component Test
patterns.summary: Test a service in isolation with all collaborators replaced by stubs.

patterns.name: Chaos Engineering
patterns.summary: Deliberately inject failures in production to validate resilience (Netflix Chaos Monkey, Gremlin).

patterns.name: Request Batch
patterns.summary: Coalesce many small requests into one network round-trip. Kafka producer batching, gRPC, DB statement batching.

patterns.name: Request Pipeline
patterns.summary: Send next request before previous response arrives; raises throughput on high-latency links. HTTP/2 multiplexing, Redis pipelining.

patterns.name: Single-Socket Channel
patterns.summary: One TCP connection between two peers carries all messages in order; preserves FIFO. Kafka inter-broker, Raft peer comms.

patterns.name: Request Waiting List
patterns.summary: Server parks a request until conditions (quorum acks, timeout, watermark) are met. Kafka purgatory, long-poll handlers.

explicit_non_overlaps_to_note: CQRS (architectural pattern: two separate models) ≠ Read-Write Split (operational: same model, route by R/W to replicas).
Choreography ≠ Pub-Sub: choreography is a saga style; pub-sub is the messaging primitive that often realizes it.
Service Mesh ≠ Sidecar: sidecar is the deployment shape; service mesh is the full system (sidecars + control plane).
Idempotent Receiver ≠ Retry: retry is the cause; idempotency is what makes retries safe.
Leader Election ≠ Leader-Follower Replication: election picks the leader; replication is what the leader does afterwards.
Optimistic/Pessimistic Locking ≠ Distributed Lock: first two are within a single DB; distributed lock is across processes/nodes.

deliberately_excluded.item: Fowler PoEAA patterns (Active Record, Data Mapper, Unit of Work, Identity Map, MVC variants, etc.)
deliberately_excluded.reason: Different scope — enterprise OO/ORM design from 2003, not cloud/distributed architecture. Catalog of 51 patterns kept as separate reference if you want it.

deliberately_excluded.item: GoF design patterns (Singleton, Observer, Factory, Strategy, etc.)
deliberately_excluded.reason: Code-level OO patterns, not system design.

deliberately_excluded.item: Protocols (HTTP, gRPC, REST, GraphQL, TCP, UDP)
deliberately_excluded.reason: Protocols, not architectural patterns. ByteByteGo covers them extensively but they're a different category.

deliberately_excluded.item: Database-internal algorithms not commonly chosen at architecture time (B-tree, Skip List)
deliberately_excluded.reason: Implementation detail of the DB you pick, not a system-design decision.

joshi_ddia_integration_notes.strategy: Joshi patterns overlapping with existing entries got 'JOS' appended to sources. Genuinely new Joshi patterns became new entries. DDIA cited as 'DDIA' on entries it conceptually covers in depth (Ch 5-9).
